trajectory_summary: The call opened with a cooperative, inbound-posture customer and a clean verify section, then stalled in a prolonged bill-navigation sequence that consumed the back half of the call without producing a booking. The rep never reached button-up. The call ended on a technical dropout with no appointment set, no kWh data confirmed, and no next-step locked. The cooperative energy Greg brought to the call was never converted.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is 'unknown' — this call should be tagged 'no_appointment_set / call_dropped_mid_bill_collection.' The customer was qualified (homeowner, credit above 670, wife on title, 7-year roof, PGE utility confirmed) and cooperative throughout. The failure is not a DQ or a lost objection — it is a process failure: the rep did not invoke the bill-collection pivot protocol, the call dropped before booking, and no callback was locked. This is a recoverable lead, not a lost one, but it requires an immediate callback with a clear plan to either capture kWh verbally or defer to the specialist.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Bill-collection pivot protocol — active direction vs. passive waiting
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I could probably find it. Let me let me get into my email.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep spent the entire back half of this call waiting passively while the wife navigated her email and utility portal without guidance. The schema's pivot protocol is explicit: when a customer cannot navigate within 1-2 attempts, the rep directs them to the kWh graph specifically or pivots to verbal readback. The rep never invoked either path. On a paid lead, this is the gating failure — no kWh data, no booking. The manager should walk the rep through the pivot triggers and the exact language for directing a customer to the usage graph, then role-play the scenario with a budget-billing customer.

key_moments.quote: K. Everything looks good over here. Great. So I just have a few questions for you, and then we can get started on a design for your home.
key_moments.stewart_read: After a 60-second unplanned hold, the rep re-enters with a confident, forward-moving frame: 'everything looks good' resolves the uncertainty the hold created, and 'get started on a design for your home' re-anchors the customer's reason for calling. This is a small but effective recovery move that kept Greg cooperative through the verify section.

Greg discloses his wife is on the title at 02:32. The rep captures this fact and moves on — no attempt to establish whether the wife needs to be present for the appointment, no scheduling question that includes her. The wife later joins the call organically (04:09) because Greg hands her the phone for bill navigation, not because the rep structured her inclusion. The appointment was never set, so the protocol violation didn't cost a booking on this call — but the pattern is present.

The wife confirms $170/month at 05:32. The rep's response — 'Oh, okay. So pretty expensive' — is a capture-and-drop. The schema's bill_anchoring pattern requires capture + flip: the bill must be turned into emotional alignment-against-the-utility, not just acknowledged as a data point. The flip was available here: the $170/month figure is the exact leverage point for 'what you're paying the utility every month is the problem solar solves.' The rep named the pain and walked away from it.

key_moments.quote: I'm on this info pen thing. So I paid the same thing every month. You can read them online.
key_moments.stewart_read: The wife discloses she is on a budget billing plan ('info pen thing' — likely 'average payment plan'). This is a distinct bill-navigation failure mode: budget billing customers pay a smoothed monthly amount that does not reflect actual kWh consumption month-to-month. The rep does not recognize this as a navigation signal — no pivot to the utility portal's usage graph, no verbal readback offer, no acknowledgment that the smoothed payment figure is not the same as kWh data. The rep continues waiting passively while the wife searches her email.
